questions.stem: This low-power section of a vessel contains an organized thrombus traversed by several new small channels. What is the functional consequence of these channels?

explanation.summary: New endothelial-lined lumina forming through an organized thrombus create channels that partially restore blood flow through the previously occluded vessel.
explanation.whyCorrect: New endothelial-lined lumina forming through an organized thrombus create channels that partially restore blood flow through the previously occluded vessel.

questions.stem: This special stain of a tissue field highlights slender rod-shaped organisms as pink/red against a blue counterstain. What structural property lets these organisms retain the stain after acid-alcohol decolorization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A polysaccharide capsule that resists phagocytosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: A thick peptidoglycan layer that traps crystal violet

choices.label: C
choices.text: Teichoic acids anchored in the plasma membrane

choices.label: E
choices.text: An outer membrane rich in lipopolysaccharide

explanation.summary: The acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen) stain highlights Mycobacterium tuberculosis because its mycolic-acid-rich waxy cell wall retains carbolfuchsin even after acid-alcohol washing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen) stain highlights Mycobacterium tuberculosis because its mycolic-acid-rich waxy cell wall retains carbolfuchsin even after acid-alcohol washing.

questions.stem: This silver-stained tissue section reveals slender black organisms with a tight corkscrew (spiral) shape. What morphologic class of pathogen is shown?

explanation.summary: Silver stains reveal the corkscrew-shaped spirochete Treponema pallidum, the causative agent of syphilis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Silver stains reveal the corkscrew-shaped spirochete Treponema pallidum, the causative agent of syphilis.

questions.stem: These microscopic fields from a syphilitic lesion show inflammation around small arteries with abundant plasma cells and, in the other panel, multinucleated giant cells. Which pair of histologic hallmarks characterizes syphilitic tissue?

explanation.summary: Syphilitic lesions show obliterative endarteritis of small vessels with a dense plasma-cell/lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, becoming granulomatous (giant cells) in late disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Syphilitic lesions show obliterative endarteritis of small vessels with a dense plasma-cell/lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, becoming granulomatous (giant cells) in late disease.

questions.stem: A silver (GMS) stain of this bronchoalveolar lavage specimen highlights dark-gray cyst walls of the causative organism. This infection is classically associated with which radiographic pattern?

explanation.summary: The frothy casts and silver-positive cyst walls identify Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, a defining opportunistic infection of AIDS. It characteristically produces bilateral interstitial infiltrates with diffuse ground-glass opacities on CT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The frothy casts and silver-positive cyst walls identify Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, a defining opportunistic infection of AIDS. It characteristically produces bilateral interstitial infiltrates with diffuse ground-glass opacities on CT.

questions.stem: This microscopic field shows a proliferation of bland, spindled cells forming slit-like vascular spaces in a patient with AIDS. Which virus drives this neoplasm?

explanation.summary: Kaposi sarcoma is composed of proliferating spindled endothelial cells and is associated with HHV-8 (also called KSHV). It is a secondary neoplasm of AIDS, presenting as cutaneous, mucosal, or visceral violaceous lesions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kaposi sarcoma is composed of proliferating spindled endothelial cells and is associated with HHV-8 (also called KSHV). It is a secondary neoplasm of AIDS, presenting as cutaneous, mucosal, or visceral violaceous lesions.

questions.stem: These malignant lymphocytes with enlarged nuclei and intermixed apoptotic bodies constitute an effusion in an AIDS patient. This B-cell malignancy is most associated with which virus?

explanation.summary: Primary effusion lymphoma is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma of body-cavity effusions associated with HHV-8 (often with EBV co-infection). Like other AIDS lymphomas it arises from viral-induced transformation in the setting of immunosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary effusion lymphoma is an aggressive B-cell lymphoma of body-cavity effusions associated with HHV-8 (often with EBV co-infection). Like other AIDS lymphomas it arises from viral-induced transformation in the setting of immunosuppression.

questions.stem: This labeled schematic maps a polymorphic gene cluster on the short arm of chromosome 6, organized into class I, class II, and class III regions. The molecules encoded here perform which primary immunologic function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forming the membrane attack complex of complement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Secreting circulating antibodies against foreign antigens

choices.label: C
choices.text: Displaying self and non-self antigens for T-cell recognition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Producing clotting factors of the coagulation cascade

explanation.summary: The diagram shows the HLA (MHC) gene complex at 6p21. These genes encode cell-surface MHC molecules that present peptide antigens, allowing the immune system to distinguish self from non-self, which is the basis of transplant matching and rejection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diagram shows the HLA (MHC) gene complex at 6p21. These genes encode cell-surface MHC molecules that present peptide antigens, allowing the immune system to distinguish self from non-self, which is the basis of transplant matching and rejection.

questions.stem: This liver biopsy shows portal lymphoplasmacytic inflammation, the defining histologic feature of chronic viral hepatitis. Which hepatotropic virus most commonly produces this chronic active hepatitis and is used as the prototype in this discussion?

explanation.summary: Hepatitis C rarely causes symptomatic acute infection but frequently becomes chronic, marked by portal lymphocytic/lymphoplasmacytic inflammation that can progress to cirrhosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatitis C rarely causes symptomatic acute infection but frequently becomes chronic, marked by portal lymphocytic/lymphoplasmacytic inflammation that can progress to cirrhosis.

questions.stem: A special stain applied to this later-stage chronic hepatitis biopsy highlights delicate bridging septa in blue. What tissue component does this blue-staining material represent?

explanation.summary: The trichrome stain colors collagen blue, highlighting the delicate bridging fibrosis that develops later in the course of chronic hepatitis C.
explanation.whyCorrect: The trichrome stain colors collagen blue, highlighting the delicate bridging fibrosis that develops later in the course of chronic hepatitis C.

questions.stem: Both rosette patterns shown here—one a radial arrangement of cells around a central fibrillar core, the other a ring of cells surrounding a true central lumen—are classically encountered in which pediatric tumor?

explanation.summary: Homer Wright rosettes (radial cells around a fibrillar center) and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes (cells around a true lumen) are both characteristic histologic features of retinoblastoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Homer Wright rosettes (radial cells around a fibrillar center) and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes (cells around a true lumen) are both characteristic histologic features of retinoblastoma.

questions.stem: This H&E field from an aggressive pediatric CNS tumor shows the dark structures indicated by the arrows. What do these structures directly indicate about the tumor?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Programmed cell death of tumor cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: A high rate of cellular proliferation

explanation.summary: The arrows point to mitotic figures (condensed dividing chromosomes staining dark purple). Abundant mitoses are a morphologic marker of rapid cellular proliferation, seen here in a medulloblastoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The arrows point to mitotic figures (condensed dividing chromosomes staining dark purple). Abundant mitoses are a morphologic marker of rapid cellular proliferation, seen here in a medulloblastoma.

questions.stem: Panel A shows normal renal tubule cells; panel B shows the same cells after an early, reversible insult. What reversible change accounts for the appearance in panel B?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cellular (hydropic) swelling from failed ion pumps

explanation.summary: Depleted ATP impairs Na+/K+ pumps, causing water influx and cellular swelling with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. This is a hallmark of reversible injury and resolves if the stimulus is removed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Depleted ATP impairs Na+/K+ pumps, causing water influx and cellular swelling with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. This is a hallmark of reversible injury and resolves if the stimulus is removed.

questions.stem: This high-power H&E field shows sheets of small round-to-oval cells with scant cytoplasm, high nuclear:cytoplasmic ratio, finely dispersed "salt-and-pepper" chromatin, and prominent nuclear molding. From which cell type does this tumor most characteristically arise?

explanation.summary: The morphology (small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, dispersed chromatin) is that of small cell lung carcinoma, a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine tumor derived from Kulchitsky cells. This origin explains its capacity for ectopic hormone production (ACTH, ADH) and neurologic paraneoplastic syndromes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The morphology (small blue cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, dispersed chromatin) is that of small cell lung carcinoma, a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine tumor derived from Kulchitsky cells. This origin explains its capacity for ectopic hormone production (ACTH, ADH) and neurologic paraneoplastic syndromes.

questions.stem: This high-power H&E field from an enlarged lymph node shows a large malignant cell (bracketed) with a bilobed 'owl-eye' nucleus and prominent eosinophilic nucleoli. This cell is diagnostic of which condition?

explanation.summary: The bilobed, owl-eye Reed-Sternberg cell with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli is the pathognomonic histologic finding of classic Hodgkin lymphoma, roughly half of which are EBV-associated.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bilobed, owl-eye Reed-Sternberg cell with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli is the pathognomonic histologic finding of classic Hodgkin lymphoma, roughly half of which are EBV-associated.

questions.stem: This histology field of a highly mitotic B-cell neoplasm shows dense sheets of neoplastic cells interspersed with scattered pale tingible body macrophages. What is this classic low-power pattern called?

explanation.summary: The pale tingible body macrophages scattered among the dark neoplastic B-cells create the 'starry sky' appearance characteristic of Burkitt lymphoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pale tingible body macrophages scattered among the dark neoplastic B-cells create the 'starry sky' appearance characteristic of Burkitt lymphoma.

questions.stem: This histologic field from an EBV-positive nasopharyngeal tumor shows nests of undifferentiated epithelial cells admixed with abundant background lymphocytes. Which histologic subtype does this represent?

explanation.summary: The EBV-associated non-keratinizing (undifferentiated) subtype of nasopharyngeal carcinoma shows undifferentiated epithelial cells with a dense lymphocytic infiltrate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The EBV-associated non-keratinizing (undifferentiated) subtype of nasopharyngeal carcinoma shows undifferentiated epithelial cells with a dense lymphocytic infiltrate.

questions.stem: This electron micrograph of a malignant lymphocyte shows a strikingly multi-lobulated, flower-shaped nucleus. This 'flower cell' is characteristic of the acute phase of which malignancy?

explanation.summary: A multi-lobulated, flower-shaped nucleus is seen in the acute phase of adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma associated with HTLV-1 infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: A multi-lobulated, flower-shaped nucleus is seen in the acute phase of adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma associated with HTLV-1 infection.

questions.stem: These gastric mucosa panels show normal antrum (left) versus mucosa colonized by H. pylori (right) that has developed a dense inflammatory infiltrate. Which inflammatory cells predominate in this chronic response?

explanation.summary: H. pylori induces chronic gastritis characterized by a mucosal infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells, the first step in the cascade toward adenocarcinoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: H. pylori induces chronic gastritis characterized by a mucosal infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells, the first step in the cascade toward adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: The right panel shows gastric mucosa that has acquired goblet cells and Paneth cells with red granules (arrows) in response to chronic H. pylori injury, compared with atrophic gastritis on the left. What is the significance of this change?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a normal variant of antral mucosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a premalignant metaplastic change

explanation.summary: Intestinal metaplasia (goblet and Paneth cells replacing gastric epithelium) is a premalignant step in the H. pylori-driven progression toward gastric adenocarcinoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intestinal metaplasia (goblet and Paneth cells replacing gastric epithelium) is a premalignant step in the H. pylori-driven progression toward gastric adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: In the H. pylori-driven gastric cascade, the left panel shows crowded, hyperchromatic nuclei (brackets) that then progress to the lesion in the right panel. What event marks this final transition?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Invasion through the basement membrane into surrounding tissue

explanation.summary: High-grade dysplasia becomes invasive gastric adenocarcinoma when the neoplastic cells invade through the basement membrane into surrounding tissues.
explanation.whyCorrect: High-grade dysplasia becomes invasive gastric adenocarcinoma when the neoplastic cells invade through the basement membrane into surrounding tissues.

questions.stem: This gastric biopsy shows a dense proliferation of small, monotonous neoplastic B-lymphocytes in the mucosa of an H. pylori-infected patient. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Extranodal marginal zone (MALT) lymphoma of the stomach is associated with H. pylori and shows an increased population of small neoplastic B-cells; many cases regress after eradication.
explanation.whyCorrect: Extranodal marginal zone (MALT) lymphoma of the stomach is associated with H. pylori and shows an increased population of small neoplastic B-cells; many cases regress after eradication.

questions.stem: This high-power field shows a single shrunken, intensely pink (eosinophilic) cell with a condensed nucleus surrounded by intact squamous cells. Which feature confirms this is apoptosis rather than necrosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lack of a surrounding inflammatory infiltrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diffuse cellular swelling of the neighboring cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of tissue architecture across the field

explanation.summary: Apoptotic cells shrink, become densely eosinophilic, and show condensed nuclei; because apoptosis is controlled and cells are cleared by phagocytes, there is little or no inflammation. Necrosis, by contrast, spills cell contents and provokes a prominent inflammatory response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Apoptotic cells shrink, become densely eosinophilic, and show condensed nuclei; because apoptosis is controlled and cells are cleared by phagocytes, there is little or no inflammation. Necrosis, by contrast, spills cell contents and provokes a prominent inflammatory response.

questions.stem: In this routinely stained section, the pink coloration of the cytoplasm is produced by which dye component?

explanation.summary: In H&E staining, eosin stains cytoplasmic and extracellular components pink while hematoxylin stains nuclei blue-purple.
explanation.whyCorrect: In H&E staining, eosin stains cytoplasmic and extracellular components pink while hematoxylin stains nuclei blue-purple.

questions.stem: This micrograph contrasts the two principal cells of acute inflammation, with a neutrophil at left and a tissue macrophage at right. Approximately when does the cell shown at right become the predominant cell at the site of injury?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediately at the moment of injury

explanation.summary: Neutrophils arrive first and peak around 24 hours; macrophages become the predominant cell type roughly 48-72 hours after injury or infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neutrophils arrive first and peak around 24 hours; macrophages become the predominant cell type roughly 48-72 hours after injury or infection.

questions.stem: This peripheral blood smear shows a single mononuclear leukocyte with a large, round, dense nucleus and only a thin rim of cytoplasm, surrounded by erythrocytes. Which leukocyte is shown?

explanation.summary: A small cell dominated by a round nucleus with scant cytoplasm is a lymphocyte. Neutrophils have multilobed nuclei, eosinophils have bilobed nuclei with red granules, monocytes are larger with reniform nuclei, and basophils contain dark blue granules.
explanation.whyCorrect: A small cell dominated by a round nucleus with scant cytoplasm is a lymphocyte. Neutrophils have multilobed nuclei, eosinophils have bilobed nuclei with red granules, monocytes are larger with reniform nuclei, and basophils contain dark blue granules.

questions.stem: This schematic depicts the organization of the extracellular matrix. The thin, specialized meshwork of type IV collagen and laminin that lies directly between the epithelial layer and the underlying interstitial connective tissue serves principally which role?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provides tensile strength and elastic recoil to tendons and ligaments

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conducts action potentials between adjacent epithelial cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stores oxygen and slowly releases it to hypoxic tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Generates contractile force that pulls wound edges together

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acts as a specialized scaffold that anchors and supports epithelial, endothelial, and smooth muscle cells

explanation.summary: The layer shown between epithelium and interstitium is the basement membrane, a specialized extracellular matrix rich in type IV collagen and laminin that provides an anchoring, supporting surface for the growth of epithelial, endothelial, and smooth muscle cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The layer shown between epithelium and interstitium is the basement membrane, a specialized extracellular matrix rich in type IV collagen and laminin that provides an anchoring, supporting surface for the growth of epithelial, endothelial, and smooth muscle cells.
